title: Tiropita
subtitle: Greek filo cheese pie with feta and eggs
excerpt: Layers of buttered filo pastry filled with a mixture of crumbled feta, eggs, and a little ricotta or cottage cheese. Baked until the filo is shatteringly crisp and golden and the filling is set. A Greek pastry that works as a starter, a main alongside salad, or as something to take to a table.
sourceNotes: Tiropita (τυρόπιτα, cheese pie) is the Greek cheese version of the filo pastry tradition shared across the Balkans and eastern Mediterranean. It appears throughout Greek cuisine both as a whole pie and as individual triangular parcels (tiropitakia). The combination of feta, eggs, and filo is documented in Greek cooking from at least the Ottoman period.

content.text: The challenge of tiropita is the filo: it dries out fast, tears when it does, and needs to be handled efficiently. Keep the stack of sheets under a just-damp cloth as you work; lay each sheet in quickly, brush with melted butter to the edges, and add the next. The filling goes in the centre layers; you need 4 or 5 sheets below and 4 or 5 above.

Feta alone produces a quite salty filling; adding ricotta or cottage cheese stretches it and makes the texture creamier. Squeeze the feta crumbles firmly, no water should remain in the cheese before you add the eggs. Score the top layers of filo with a sharp knife before baking: this allows steam to escape and gives you clean slices afterwards.

items.name: Filo pastry
items.prepNote: thawed if frozen, kept covered with a damp cloth

items.name: Feta
items.prepNote: crumbled and squeezed of excess brine

content.text: Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Combine the crumbled {{feta}} and {{ricotta}} in a bowl. Beat the {{eggs}} and stir through the cheese mixture. Season with {{black-pepper}} and a pinch of {{nutmeg-ground}}. Taste before adding any salt, the feta provides most of the salt the filling needs.

Brush a 30 cm × 20 cm baking tin with melted {{unsalted-butter}}. Lay a sheet of {{filo-pastry}} in the tin, brush with butter, and repeat with 4 more sheets. Spread the filling evenly over the layered filo. Lay the remaining 5 sheets on top, buttering each one, and tuck the edges down the sides of the tin. Score the top 2 or 3 sheets in a diamond or square pattern with a sharp knife. Brush the top generously with butter.

Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until the filo is deep golden and the filling is set when the tin is gently shaken. Rest for 10 minutes before cutting along the scored lines. Serve warm or at room temperature, tiropita is also good cold.

items.fix: Work under a damp cloth; move quickly once uncovered; torn sheets still work, layer over the tear with the next sheet and butter over
Butter the tin first; drain the ricotta; squeeze the feta; bake on the lower shelf of the oven
items.cause: Tin not pre-buttered, or filling too wet

items.fix: The filling should be just set with a very slight wobble; 40 minutes is the maximum, check from 35 minutes
items.symptom: Filling is rubbery and set too hard

items.fix: Never add salt without tasting first; squeeze the feta before using; some feta varieties are saltier than others
items.cause: Feta not brine-squeezed, and salt added on top

content.text: Tiropita belongs to the wide family of filo pastry pies shared across the Balkans and eastern Mediterranean. Spanakopita (spinach and feta), spanakotiropita (spinach and cheese), bourek across Turkey and the old Ottoman lands, and byrek in Albania all share the same shape. The common thread is filo layered with butter or oil and filled with cheese, meat, or veg. In Greece, tiropita is sold from bakeries as both a whole pie and as small triangles (tiropitakia). People eat them for breakfast or as a snack. The whole pie is a home dish; the triangle is the bakery one.
